title: Flutter Junior Developer Mock Test
description: Assess foundational Flutter knowledge for junior developers covering widgets, state, navigation, layouts, assets, and core framework concepts.

questions.text: Which widget is the root of a typical Flutter application?
questions.explanation: MaterialApp is usually the root widget that configures the top-level Navigator, theme, and routes.

questions.text: What is the difference between a StatelessWidget and a StatefulWidget?
options: StatelessWidget can have mutable state, StatefulWidget cannot
StatefulWidget can rebuild when its internal state changes; StatelessWidget cannot
StatelessWidget is only for layout, StatefulWidget for logic
questions.explanation: StatefulWidget maintains a State object that can change over time, triggering rebuilds. StatelessWidget is immutable.
hints: Which one holds data that can change?

questions.text: How do you update the UI when the internal state of a StatefulWidget changes?
questions.explanation: Calling setState() marks the widget as dirty and schedules a rebuild of the subtree.
hints: Accepts a callback where you change variables

questions.text: Which widget provides a basic visual layout structure with an app bar, body, and floating action button?
questions.explanation: Scaffold implements the basic Material Design visual layout structure and provides slots for appBar, body, FAB, drawer, etc.

options: Only the app's name and description
Project metadata, dependencies, assets, and fonts
questions.explanation: pubspec.yaml defines the package name, version, dependencies (like flutter SDK), dev dependencies, assets, fonts, and other metadata.
hints: It's required in every Flutter project

questions.text: How do you display an image stored in the `assets/images/` folder?
questions.explanation: Image.asset loads an image from the app's asset bundle. The asset must be declared in pubspec.yaml.
hints: Must be listed in pubspec.yaml under assets

questions.text: Which widget is used to create a scrollable list of children?
questions.explanation: ListView is a scrollable list of widgets arranged linearly. It can be used for both small and large lists.

questions.text: What is the purpose of the `build` method in a widget?
options: To construct the widget's state object
To describe the widget's UI in terms of other widgets
questions.explanation: The build method returns a widget tree that describes the UI. It is called whenever the widget needs to be rendered or rebuilt.
hints: Called when the widget is first created and after setState

questions.text: What is the difference between hot reload and hot restart?
options: Hot reload preserves state, hot restart resets the app to initial state
Hot reload restarts the whole VM, hot restart only updates UI
questions.explanation: Hot reload injects updated code into the running Dart VM while preserving app state. Hot restart fully resets the app state.
hints: One keeps your variables, the other doesn't
Think about what happens to text fields when you press each

questions.text: Which method is called only once when a StatefulWidget's State object is created?
questions.explanation: initState() is called exactly once when the State object is inserted into the tree. It is used for one-time initializations.
hints: Use it to initialize controllers or subscriptions

questions.text: How do you navigate to a new screen and back in Flutter?
questions.explanation: Navigator manages a stack of routes. push adds a route, pop removes the current route.

questions.text: What is the purpose of a `FutureBuilder` widget?
options: To build widgets that depend on the result of a Future
questions.explanation: FutureBuilder takes a Future and a builder function. It rebuilds when the Future completes, showing appropriate UI for loading/error/data states.

questions.text: Which widget would you use to add space between widgets in a Column?
questions.explanation: SizedBox(height: 20) is common, but Padding around a child or a Container with margin also adds space. All can be used depending on the context.

questions.text: In Dart (and Flutter), how do you make a variable non-nullable?
options: Add a '!' after the variable name
Declare it without '?' and initialize it with a non-null value
questions.explanation: By default, types are non-nullable. You must either initialize the variable or use the 'late' keyword to promise initialization before use.

questions.text: What is the purpose of `dispose()` method in a State class?
options: To free up resources when the widget is removed from the tree
questions.explanation: dispose() is called when the State object is permanently removed. Use it to cancel streams, dispose controllers, etc.
